questions.question: What is the purpose of the #pragma omp task directive in OpenMP? How does it differ from #pragma omp parallel for?
questions.hint: Consider when loop parallelism is insufficient
keyPoints: Creates a explicit task - a unit of work scheduled by runtime
Task is deferrer - executed later by any available thread
Unlike parallel for - tasks handle irregular/non-loop workloads
Enables dynamic load balancing via work-stealing
Tasks can have dependencies, unlike simple parallel for

questions.question: Explain the function of #pragma omp taskwait in OpenMP tasking. When is it necessary?
questions.hint: Think about data dependency between parent and child tasks
keyPoints: Synchronization directive - waits for all child tasks to complete
Creates implicit task barrier
Needed when parent task requires results from child tasks
Can be used inside parallel regions or single regions
Does not wait for descendant tasks (only immediate children)

questions.question: Write an OpenMP program using tasking to compute the Fibonacci sequence recursively. Explain how task dependencies are handled.
questions.hint: Show both task creation and synchronization
keyPoints: Base case: fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1
Create tasks for recursive calls using #pragma omp task
Use taskwait after creating both recursive tasks
Dependency: tasks must complete before parent can compute sum
May use task depend clause for in/out dependencies
Task granularity affects performance

questions.question: Compare task-based parallelism with loop-based parallelism in OpenMP. When would you choose one over the other?
questions.hint: Consider the nature of the algorithm structure
keyPoints: Loop parallelism: fixed iteration count, regular workloads
Task parallelism: irregular, recursive, or dynamic workloads
Loop parallelism: simpler, less overhead
Task parallelism: more flexible, handles complex dependencies
Choose loop for parallel for, choose task for divide-and-conquer

questions.question: Given the following OpenMP code with 4 threads, trace the execution: #pragma omp parallel {#pragma omp single {#pragma omp task {process A();}#pragma omp task {process B();}#pragma omp taskwait; process C();}} How many tasks are created? Which thread executes which task?
questions.hint: Remember task is deferred, single executes immediately
keyPoints: 3 tasks created (A, B, C)
Tasks A and B created in single region - deferred execution
Any of the 4 threads may execute A or B (runtime scheduling)
taskwait ensures A and B complete before C executes
C executes in single region (one thread)
Thread executing single creates tasks but may not execute them

questions.question: Explain the concept of task dependencies in OpenMP using the task depend clause. How does this enable producer-consumer patterns?
questions.hint: Think about data flow between tasks
keyPoints: Three dependency types: in, out, inout
out/inout: task produces data
in: task consumes data produced by another
Runtime ensures dependent tasks execute in correct order
Producer-consumer: producer task has out, consumer has in
Eliminates need for explicit taskwait in some cases
Enables fine-grained synchronization

questions.question: What is the purpose of #pragma omp taskgroup in OpenMP? How does it differ from taskwait?
keyPoints: taskgroup waits for all tasks within the group to complete
Includes nested/tasks created by descendant tasks
taskwait only waits for immediate child tasks
taskgroup creates a structured block
Useful for recursive algorithms where full tree must complete

questions.question: Analyze the following code for potential issues: #pragma omp parallel {#pragma omp for for(int i=0; i<n; i++) {#pragma omp task {process(arr[i]);}}} Identify the problem and suggest a fix.
questions.hint: Consider what happens when tasks access shared data
keyPoints: Problem: task inside parallel for creates tasks for each iteration
Each task accesses arr[i] - shared array
No synchronization for array access
Tasks may execute concurrently causing data races
Fix: Use task depend or add appropriate synchronization
Alternative: Use combined #pragma omp parallel for instead
May need firstprivate for loop index

questions.question: Design an OpenMP task-based solution for parallelizing tree traversal (post-order). Show the code and explain the task synchronization strategy.
keyPoints: Define recursive function with node processing
Create tasks for left and right subtree traversal
Use taskwait after both child tasks
Process current node after children complete
Use if clause to avoid creating too many small tasks
Consider final task for leaf nodes (no task creation)

questions.question: What is task priority in OpenMP? How does the priority clause affect task execution?
questions.hint: Consider which tasks should execute first
keyPoints: priority clause sets relative priority of tasks
Higher priority tasks scheduled first when thread becomes available
Value range: 0 (default) to highest
Helps prioritize critical path tasks
Runtime may still ignore priority under heavy load

questions.question: Calculate the minimum number of threads needed and estimate speedup for a task-based quicksort implementation with n=100000 elements. Given that task creation overhead is 10μs and task execution time is 100μs, estimate the overhead percentage.
questions.hint: Consider number of tasks vs available threads
keyPoints: Quicksort creates O(n) tasks in worst case
100000 tasks created
Total creation overhead: 100000 × 10μs = 1 second
Total execution time: 100000 × 100μs = 10 seconds (sequential)
With infinite threads: parallel time ≈ 0.1 seconds + overhead
Overhead percentage: 1/(1+10) ≈ 9%
Practical threads limited, so speedup depends on thread count

questions.question: Explain the concept of task affinity in OpenMP. How does the affinity clause improve performance in NUMA architectures?
questions.hint: Think about data locality and memory access patterns
keyPoints: Affinity controls where tasks execute relative to data
affinity(modifier: list) specifies preferred thread binding
Important for NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) systems
Reduces memory latency by placing tasks near their data
Modifiers: thread, core, socket
Improves cache utilization
Can hurt performance if incorrectly specified

questions.question: What is the role of the OpenMP runtime in task scheduling? Explain work-stealing behavior.
questions.hint: Consider what happens when threads have different workloads
keyPoints: Runtime maintains task queue per thread
When thread has no work, it steals from other queues
Work-stealing balances load dynamically
Tasks created but not executed are stored in queue
Improves utilization when workload is unbalanced
Stealing thread takes from bottom of queue (newest tasks)

questions.question: Write a complete OpenMP program using tasking to parallelize merge sort. Include proper task synchronization and explain how you would optimize for performance.
keyPoints: Recursive function for divide step
Create tasks for left and right halves
Use taskwait after recursive calls
Merge is sequential (or can be parallelized further)
Use if(n > threshold) to avoid excessive task creation
Consider final tasks for small subarrays
Use firstprivate for boundaries
Task priority for larger subproblems
